在过去的一年中，对冲基金超级大佬通过出售他们并不真正拥有的Gametop股票来赚钱，他们只是借来的。卖空。如果他们卖出足够多的股票，就可以将价格压低到如此之高，以至于当他们最终需要归还所借入的股票时，便可以使它们变得便宜。它是免费的钱。他们为此付出了数亿美元的资金，他们在办公室里冷落地观看无家可归者的实时视频，这些视频无家可归，他们被困在老式跑车的引擎盖上，写了一份投资者报告，并将其称为会计年度。
  他们借入并卖出了创纪录的数量-实际上，他们卖出的股票数量超过了实际交易的数量，远远超过了Gametop的流通量。这不应该被允许发生，并且可能意味着他们正在出售他们甚至不愿借钱的股票-裸短裤。 （SEC，您在那儿呢？）本质上，他们同时押注Gametop破产，并尽最大努力使他们破产。当您需要找到一种方式来支付老婆的a养费和新妻子的男服务员时，这是一个很好的策略。
  但这为精明的堕落赌徒提供了机会。因为这些股票最终需要归还-一方面，它们只能被借用一定天数，而另一方面，要借入这些资金会花费这些资金。这意味着他们在某个时候需要回购所出售的借入股票。
    因此，如果我们有远见的赌徒投资于Gamestop，他们的股票将得到保证的购买者。如果数百万堕落的同胞要求他们的母亲预支津贴，以便他们也可以购买Gametop，
  然后，如果一位著名的电子商务首席执行官打算购买一吨Gametop股份，加入董事会，并宣布他们将通过做互联网，电子竞技和新奇事物再次成为大公司-
  然后，如果真正疯狂的看涨期权购买量-如果您不知道，则不必担心-迫使做市商迅速购买大量股票以履行他们出售的所有这些期权这是一种疯狂现象，基本上相当于大气微爆发的股票（称为伽马挤压），突然使Gametop的价格飙升到前所未有的水平-
    好了，您就拥有了短暂挤压的理想条件。价格高得惊人。那里有一整条资金雄厚的资金，他们绝对必须找到一种方法来购买所有这些疯狂的昂贵股票。通过购买-交易量的140％！ -他们将进一步提高价格。因此，有一天，足够多的借入股票将到期，基金将不得不通过回购价格来推动GME的价格上涨。随着价格的上涨，将要求其他空头持有人通过购买股票来偿还所借股票。这是一种失控的反应，发生的次数越多，发生的次数就越多。您知道，像病毒传播那样的可爱现象之一。还是核弹。 
那么他们从谁那里买呢？没错。他们以什么价格购买？好吧，这取决于。
  持有GME空头的对冲基金经理真的很想说服GME股票持有人立即卖出一些股票，然后再攀升，以便他们可以归还借来的股票并在破产之前破产。而且他们有很多工具可以用来做到这一点：他们可以吸引其他股票来创建FOMO，从而导致GME持有人出售其股票以追逐一些闪亮的新模因。他们可以雇用公关公司在埃隆·马斯克（Elon Musk）的粉丝俱乐部和赌博论坛上对这些股票进行投资。他们可以购买股票，然后在交易时间结束后，以逐渐便宜的价格出售股票，以压低股价。他们可以抹掉自己身上的流浪汉的鲜血，然后在电视上大哭大叫。实际上，他们已经尝试了所有这些方法。但是它没有用-GME的价格比以往更高。现在，它已经失控了-涉及的人太多。模因达到了临界质量。
  现在，这是一个经典的百万玩家囚徒困境：每个GME持有者都有愿景，希望以无限的鸡肉趋势和可卡因蘸酱出售其股票。也许他们认为自己一个人就能卖出，而其他所有人都可以通过持有来继续抬高价格。但是，如果每个堕落的赌徒都这样想，并卖掉了自己的股票，很快就不会发生短暂的挤兑。空头持有人将以痛苦但可控制的损失买入所有出售的股票，他们将弥补头寸，而核武器永远不会被引爆。
  在这个困境中的囚犯该怎么办？最后，要点到了。为了避免过早卖出，精明的堕落赌徒会等到空头兴趣（那里卖空的股票数量）开始大幅下降。只要没有人投降，就没有人卖得早，卖空的股票下跌将伴随着股票价格的飙升，因为很少的可用股票是以天文价格购买的。卖空股票的减少将使这种上涨与伽玛紧缩或常规旧股暴涨区分开来。
  然后，直到那时候，随着核武器的消失，股价上升到半人马座阿尔法（Alpha Centauri），空头兴趣终于开始下降，空头挤压开始了。然后是每个赌徒自己。
note_en:
Over the past year, hedge fund supervillains have made money by selling shares of Gamestop they don't actually own - they've just borrowed them. Short selling. If they sell enough they can drive the price down so far that when they eventually need to return the shares they borrowed, they can get them cheap. It's free money. They throw a couple hundred mil at this, chill in their offices watching live video feeds of homeless people being exsanguinated on the hoods of their vintage sports cars, write up an investor report, and call it a fiscal year.
  They borrowed and sold a record amount - they sold more shares, in fact, than are actually traded, far more than Gamestop's float. This shouldn't have been allowed to happen and probably means they were selling shares they never even bothered to borrow - naked shorts. (Where were you on that one, SEC?) Essentially, they were simultaneously betting on Gamestop going bankrupt and doing their best to drive them into bankruptcy. It's a good tactic when you need to find a way to pay for your old wife's alimony and your new wife's poolboy.
  But it presents an opportunity for the savvy degenerate gambler. Because these shares eventually need to be returned - for one thing, they can only be borrowed for a certain number of days, and for another, it costs these funds money to borrow a share. This means that at some point, they need to buy back those borrowed shares they sold.
    So our visionary gambler, if they were to invest in Gamestop, would have a guaranteed buyer for their shares. And if millions of fellow degenerates were to ask their mother for an advance on their allowance so that they could buy Gamestop, too -
  And then, if a famous e-commerce CEO were to buy a ton of Gamestop shares, join the board, and announce they're going to be a big company again by doing internet things and esports and radical new stuff -
  And then, if a truly insane amount of call option buying - don't worry about it if you don't know - were to force market makers to rapidly buy up a ton of shares to fulfill all those options they sold in a wild phenomenon that's basically the stock equivalent of an atmospheric microburst, called a gamma squeeze, suddenly spiking Gamestop's price to unheard-of levels -
    Well then you'd have the perfect conditions for a short squeeze. The price is insanely high. There's a whole street of funds with deep pockets who absolutely must find a way to buy all those insanely expensive shares. And by buying them - 140% of the amount traded! - they're going to drive up the price even more. So one day, a large enough block of borrowed shares will expire that a fund will have to drive GME's price through the roof in buying them back. And as the price climbs, other short holders are going to be required to cover their borrowed shares by buying them. It's a runaway reaction where the more it happens, the more it happens. You know, one of those cute little phenomena like virus spread. Or nuclear bombs.
  So who are they buying from? That's right. At what price are they buying? Well, that depends.
  Hedge fund managers holding GME shorts would really, really like to convince GME stock holders to sell them some shares right now, before it climbs any higher, so that they can return the shares they borrowed and get out before they get steamrolled into bankrupcy. And they've got lots of tools at their disposal to do this: they can pump up other stocks to create FOMO, causing GME holders to sell their shares to go chase some shiny new meme. They can hire PR companies to astroturf these stocks on Elon Musk fan clubs and gambling forums. They can buy up shares and then, after trading hours are over, sell them in progressively cheaper tranches to drive down the stock price. They can wipe the hobo blood off their wattle and go cry on television about how they're being bullied. They have, in fact, tried all of these things. But it hasn't worked - GME's price is higher than ever. It's out of control, now - there are too many people involved. The meme has reached critical mass.
  Now it's a classic million-player prisoner's dilemma: every GME holder has visions of selling their shares for unlimited chicken tendies and cocaine dipping sauce. Maybe they think they alone can sell, while everyone else can continue to drive the price up by holding. But if every degenerate gambler thought this way, and sold their shares, very quickly the short squeeze wouldn't happen. Short holders would buy up all the shares being sold at a painful but manageable loss, they would cover their position, and the nuke would never be detonated.
  What's a prisoner in this dilemma to do? At last, the point arrives. To avoid selling too early, the savvy degenerate gambler would wait until short interest - the amount of shares shorted out there - started to decline substantially. As long as nobody was defecting, nobody selling early, that decline in shares shorted would come with a spike in the price of the stock, as the few shares available are bought at astronomical prices. And this decline in shares shorted would distinguish this spike from gamma squeezes or regular old stock run-ups.
  Then and only then, as the nuke goes off, the stock price ascends past Alpha Centauri, and the short interest finally starts declining, the short squeeze has begun. And then it's every gambler for themself.
title: 挤压被挤压了吗？
title_en: Has the Squeeze Been Squoze Yet?

note:
到1959年2月下旬，救援队用直升机直升到偏远的Dyatlov Pass时，九名俄罗斯冒险家—七男两女，都是经验丰富的越野滑雪者—已经死亡了近一个月。关于现场的一切似乎都不对。冒险家的帐篷从内部切成薄片，在其果壳上放有帆布背包，整齐排列的靴子和一盘切成薄片的猪肉脂肪。救援人员在离营地半英里的下坡处发现了受难者，其中一些人赤脚几乎赤身露体。死亡的主要原因是体温过低-他们逃离的夜晚的温度将远远低于华氏零度-但其中两个死者失去了眼睛，另一只则失去了舌头。四个人的头部和胸部遭受了严重的创伤，就好像他们在车祸中一样。这些伤害并非与雪崩死亡相符。
 几十年来，所谓的Dyatlov Pass事件引起了许多阴谋论。一定是外星人使俄国人逃到了冰冷的死亡，事实证明，一些冒险家的衣服上有放射性痕迹。或者雪人偶然发现了营地。或者，更合理地说，当地人不喜欢该组织入侵他们的土地。最后，尽管所有这些奇怪的情况，这些都没有特别令人信服的俄罗斯政府正式将雪崩归咎于罪魁祸首。
 60多年后的现在，科学家们说，他们有新的证据来支持这种说法，但有一个转折点：杀手可能是一种奇特的雪崩。受先前为迪斯尼电影《冰雪奇缘》模拟现实雪景的工作的启发，研究人员模拟了一个相对较小的雪崩如何袭击营地，迫使冒险者逃离并严重伤害其中一些人。
    1959年2月1日夜幕降临之前的某个时候，越野滑雪者建立了一个简单的营地，只是一个帐篷，他们所有人挤在一起睡着了。我们之所以知道这个时间表，是因为调查人员在白天恢复了小组的相机并找到了倾斜帐篷的图像。我们还知道，救援队在夜间迅速撤离，帐篷的割伤证明了这一点（如果被困的人无法呼吸，也许会疯狂地撤离），并且救援队还在庇护所附近恢复了灯笼。
  至于露营者的死亡时间，研究人员有一些证据。根据最近的气象站的读数，在如此寒冷的天气（估计为–13华氏度）下，如果没有人体加热来保暖，一个人的手表在死亡后约一个小时会停止滴答。其中三只冒险者的手表在上午8点至上午9点之间停下，另一只手表则在5:31停下来。温度还决定着暴露的人穿很少衣服的最大生存时间。在这种情况下，窗口应该持续两到三个小时。最后，受害人的胃部内容显示自从他们进餐以来已经六到八个小时了。
  放在一起，滑雪者在夜幕降临之前就扎营，很可能在凌晨1:30至5:30逃离，并在凌晨4:30至7:30死亡。
 如果您打算进行一次典型的雪崩，那没有什么意义。一方面，救援队没有看到任何大雪的迹象-他们很容易发现帐篷，而且帐篷没有被深深掩埋。另外，帐篷周围的平均倾斜度为23度，远低于科学家告诉您触发雪崩所需的30度倾斜度。冒险家最初建立营地时，就是在雪地上砍平空间。但是调查人员确定，露营者直到进行砍伐后至少九小时才逃离。如果割伤引发了雪崩，则该滑坡应该立即发生。 
不过，对于科学家约翰·高梅（Johan Gaume）和亚历山大·普兹林（Alexander Puzrin）来说，这确实是有道理的。他们今天在《通讯地球与通讯》杂志上阐述了他们对Dyatlov Pass事件的理论。环境。 （顺便说一句，这是我读过的第一篇科学论文，提到了“雪人的进攻”。）他们认为，这种状况很可能催生了所谓的延迟平板雪崩。
 当今年的第一场雪落下时，它会与仍然相对温暖的地面接触。但是空气温度急剧下降，产生了一个温度梯度，该温度梯度形成了多孔晶体材料（称为薄层），即80％的空气。最重要的是，更多的雪落下来，形成了更致密的平板。可以把它想象成一个停车库，其中薄弱的一层是停车位和稀疏的柱子（很多通风的空间）。上方的实心天花板是楼板。现在，如果您以某种方式打扰该薄弱的层，将那些柱子击倒，它将塌陷，将其上方的平板作为雪崩释放。
  或想一想当您将两本书放在手掌上然后倾斜时会发生什么：第一本书到达临界角时滑落。在平板雪崩期间，同样的事情也会发生在那顶密集的雪层上。它直接滑离下层。
 对于我们的情况而言，至关重要的是，平板雪崩触发时不需要30度的坡度，而更像是20度。冒险者营地上方的平均坡度为23度，调查人员发现，在Dyatlov Pass事件发生时，当地积雪的底部很薄。
  越野滑雪者实际上已经在山坡上的一小段山坡上扎营，铲起积雪将其平整。当他们切入积雪堆时，会切穿薄弱的一层，从本质上开始倒数计时。瑞士联邦理工学院的雪物理学家Gaume说：“当您在斜坡上开凿切口以安装帐篷时，就像拆除挡土墙一样。”现在，那片密密的积雪摇摇欲坠地笼罩在营地上。 “所有食材都在那里，” Gaume补充道。 “有一个薄弱的层，有一个平板，并且倾斜角局部比临界角陡。”
 但是为什么板子在帐篷坠落前悬挂在上面呢？按照露营者的死亡时间，按研究人员的计算，板子在这里悬挂了9.5到13.5小时？这与典型的雪崩完全不同，后者在发生干扰（例如不幸的滑雪板）后会立即崩溃。 “在极少数情况下，您将炸药扔到山坡上引发雪崩，然后实际上是雪崩释放了，比如说半小时后，或类似的情况，”雪崩科学家JürgSchweizer说没有参与这项新工作的瑞士联邦森林，雪地和景观研究所。 “然后，通常用这样的解释来解释这些雪崩：‘好吧，也许是您造成了亚临界故障，然后在一段时间内发展或增长到临界尺寸，从而扩展了裂纹。”
  延迟的平板雪崩是另一种野兽。营地周围的薄弱层显然仍然足够坚固，可以在最初的几个小时内支撑积雪，但是还有另一个因素在起作用。高梅（Gaume）和普兹林（Puzrin）从对该事件的调查中得知，当时的气象站已经检测到强烈的“ katabatic”风，这是从希腊语中提取的一个词，意思是“往下走”。这些风确实做到了：在重力的作用下，它们迅速从丘陵和山脉的顶部下降，从更高的高度搜寻积雪并将其沉积在下方。 
研究人员模拟了这样的风如何在帐篷上方积雪，以及达到临界载荷需要多长时间才能使顶板从下面的较弱层滑落，因为其结构完整性受到了切。 “这就是负载增加的方式，” Gaume说。 “因为别无选择，那天晚上没有降雪。”午夜后的某个时候，薄弱的层之上已经积聚了足够的重量，以至于它突然坍塌，将厚板送入帐篷。这将是一个相对较小的雪崩-可能是16英尺x 16英尺-研究人员在迪斯尼雪场模型的启发下进行了模拟。足以填补营员在雪地里挖的洞，但还不足以使救援队能够在26天后找到雪崩的明显迹象。
  不过，雪崩不必很大，以免对人体造成严重伤害。通常，陷入其中的远足者可能会窒息而死。但是在这种情况下，九名受害者中没有一人死于窒息，有些还遭受了严重的胸部和头部创伤。
 这也可以用平板雪崩的动力学和向下的风来解释。事件发生时虽然没有下雪，但四方风会在帐篷上方产生更加危险的沉积物。高梅说：“风在侵蚀并运输由很小的晶体制成的雪。” “然后当它沉积时，[晶体]被高度压实。”这可能会造成一块厚厚的积雪，每立方英尺可能重达25磅​​。对于我们的冒险家来说，更不幸的是，他们将滑雪板摆成帐篷的地板，为雪压下了坚硬的基底。
 Gaume和Puzrin进一步建模了这种创伤的模样。为了校准仿真，他们使用了旧的汽车行业碰撞测试中的数据，这些测试是使用人体尸体而不是假人完成的。 （公平地说，那是1970年代，那是一个……不同的时间。）然后，他们将不同大小的模拟积雪的释放建模到人体的数字模型上，并将其与碰撞测试结果进行了比较。高梅说：“我们看到的是，这不会致命，但会造成中度至严重的伤害。” （在下面，您可以看到一米多的积雪可能造成的损害。）
  由此得出的结论是，登山者在最初的积雪中幸免于难，从帐篷中脱出了路，尽管其中一些人受了重伤。但是，如果他们逃脱了一次相对较小的雪崩，为什么他们逃离半英里以外的地方，而不是四处寻找他们的补给品，尤其是靴子？调查人员发现，该小组实际上已经在森林中藏匿了另一组补给品，因此他们可能会惊慌地出发。高梅说：“您开始从内部切开帐篷出去。” “您看到那里发生了雪崩，然后您可能会担心再次发生雪崩。因此，他们可能已经决定，最好的选择可能是去森林里，生火，然后设法找到供应源。”
  但是，他们穿着小衣服，在严酷的寒冷中存活了不到几个小时。他们无法得知平板雪崩的复杂动态，而且，尽管震惊不已，但挖掘出他们的物资并继续前进可能是安全的。
 蒙大拿州立大学雪崩科学家乔迪·亨德里克斯（Jordy Hendrikx）说：“我确实确实认为雪崩是这种情况的最合理的解释。”他没有参与这项新工作。 “我认为令人兴奋的是像Johan [Gaume]这样的人，他拥有的技能和他开发模型的方式，可以为我们提供更多的见解。” 
早在1959年，研究人员对雪崩如何发挥作用的了解还有限。他们没有意识到，例如，在适当的条件下，雪崩在小于30度的坡度上滑动是完全合理的。因此有一段时间，当局怀疑当地人民谋杀了冒险家。而且，如果雪崩看起来似乎不是特别合理，那为什么不考虑超自然现象呢？ “我认为外星人和雪人是有趣的想法，”亨德里克斯说。 “尤其是在有时间的情况下，苏联和最近的俄罗斯在信息公开方面并不出名。因此，我可以理解为什么这些荒唐的理论汇聚在一起。”
 尽管这种新的平板雪崩理论实际上对它具有科学上的严格性，但实际上它仍然是：一种理论。毕竟，没有目击者幸存。高梅说：“我想明确地说，我们并不声称我们解决了这个谜团。” “我的意思是，没人想在俄罗斯解决这个谜。这是俄罗斯绝杀的一部分。”
   Gear利用我们Gear团队的最佳选择，优化您的家庭生活，从机器人吸尘器到负担得起的床垫再到智能扬声器
note_en:
By the time the rescue team helicoptered to the remote Dyatlov Pass in late February 1959, the nine Russian adventurers—seven men and two women, all highly experienced cross-country skiers—had been dead for nearly a month. Nothing about the scene seemed right. The adventurers’ tent had been sliced open from the inside, and in its husk lay rucksacks, neatly arranged boots, and a plate of sliced pork fat. The rescuers found the victims themselves over half a mile downslope from their camp, some of them barefoot and almost naked. The primary cause of death was hypothermia—temperatures would have been well below zero degrees Fahrenheit the night they fled—but two of the deceased were missing their eyes, and another her tongue. Four had suffered severe trauma to their heads and chests, as if they’d been in a car crash. These were not injuries consistent with a death by avalanche.
 Over the decades, what became known as the  Dyatlov Pass incident has prompted many a conspiracy theory. It must have been aliens that made the Russians flee to an icy death, as evidenced by the fact that some of the adventurers’ clothes bore traces of radioactivity. Or a Yeti had stumbled upon the camp. Or, more plausibly, the local humans didn’t appreciate the group’s intrusion on their lands. In the end, none of these were particularly convincing to the Russian government, which officially blamed an avalanche as the culprit, all those curious circumstances notwithstanding.
 Now, more than 60 years later, scientists say they’ve got new evidence to back up that claim, but with a twist: The killer was probably a peculiar  kind of avalanche. Inspired by previous work that modeled realistic snow for the Disney film  Frozen, the researchers simulated how a  relatively tiny avalanche could have struck the camp, forcing the adventurers to flee, and severely injuring some of them.
    Sometime before nightfall on February 1, 1959, the cross-country skiers had made a simple camp, just one tent into which they’d all crammed side by side to sleep. We know this timeline because investigators recovered the group’s cameras and found an image of the pitched tent in daylight. We also know that the group evacuated quickly at night, as evidenced by the cut in the tent—perhaps done frantically, if people trapped inside couldn’t breathe—and the fact that the rescue team also recovered lanterns near the shelter.
  As for the campers’ time of death, researchers have a few lines of evidence. Without body heat to keep it warm, in such cold weather—estimated to be –13 degrees F, based on readings from the closest weather stations—a person’s watch stops ticking about an hour after their death. Three of the adventurers’ watches stopped between 8 am and 9 am, and another’s stopped at 5:31 am. The temperature also determines the maximum survival time for an exposed human being wearing little clothing; in this case, the window should have been two to three hours. Lastly, the victims’ stomach contents showed it’d been six to eight hours since their last meals.
  Putting this all together, the skiers pitched camp before nightfall, likely fled between 1:30 am and 5:30 am, and perished between 4:30 am and 7:30 am.
 If you're envisioning a typical avalanche, this doesn’t make much sense. For one thing, the rescue team didn’t see any sign of a massive movement of snow—they had easily spotted the tent, and it was not deeply buried. Plus, the slope around the tent was 23 degrees on average, well below the 30 degree incline that scientists will tell you it takes to trigger an avalanche. When the adventurers had originally set up camp, they’d cut into the snow to level out a space. Yet investigators determined that the campers hadn’t fled until at least nine hours after making that cut. If the cut had triggered an avalanche, that slide should have happened immediately.
  This all  does make sense, though, to scientists Johan Gaume and Alexander Puzrin, who laid out their theory for the Dyatlov Pass incident  today in the journal  Communications Earth & Environment. (It is, incidentally, the first scientific paper I’ve ever read that mentions “attacks by Yetis.”) The conditions, they argue, could well have spawned what’s known as a delayed slab avalanche.
 When the year's first snow falls, it comes in contact with ground that’s still relatively warm. But the air temperature has fallen dramatically, creating a temperature gradient that builds a porous crystalline material, known as a weak layer, that’s 80 percent air. On top of this, more snow falls, forming a denser slab. Think of it like a parking garage, with the weak layer being the parking spaces and the sparse pillars—lots of airy space. The solid ceiling above is the slab. Now if you somehow disturb that weak layer, knocking out those pillars, it’ll collapse, releasing the slab above it as an avalanche.
  Or think of what happens when you stack two books on your palm, then tilt them: The top book slides off once it reaches a critical angle. During a slab avalanche, the same thing happens with that top layer of denser snow; it slides right off the lower layer.
 Critically for our scenario, a slab avalanche doesn’t require a 30 degree slope to trigger it—it’s more like 20 degrees. The average slope above the adventurers’ camp was 23 degrees, and investigators found that at the time of the Dyatlov Pass incident, the base of the local snowpack was weak.
  The cross-country skiers had actually pitched camp on a small step in the hillside, scooping away the snow to level it out. When they cut into the snowpack, they sliced through the weak layer, essentially initiating a countdown. “When you create a cut in the slope to install the tent, it's like when you remove a retaining wall,” says Gaume, a snow physicist at the Swiss Federal Institute of Technology. The slab of denser snow now hung precariously over the camp. “All the ingredients were there,” Gaume adds. “There was a weak layer, there was a slab, and the slope angle was locally steeper than the critical angle.”
 But why did the slab hang there—for between 9.5 and 13.5 hours by the researchers’ calculation, given the campers’ times of death—before crashing through the tent? This is very unlike a typical avalanche, which smashes down immediately after a disturbance, for instance an unfortunate snowboarder. “Very rarely there are cases when you throw explosives into a slope to trigger an avalanche, and then actually the avalanche releases, let's say half an hour later, or something like that,” says Jürg Schweizer, an avalanche scientist at the Swiss Federal Institute for Forest, Snow and Landscape Research, who wasn’t involved in this new work. “And then those avalanches are typically explained by saying, ‘Well, maybe you caused a subcritical failure and that then developed or grew within some time to critical size for crack propagation.’”
  The delayed slab avalanche in question is a different beast. The weak layer around the camp was apparently still strong enough to initially support the snow for several hours, but there was another factor at play. Gaume and Puzrin knew from investigations of the incident that at the time weather stations had detected strong “katabatic” winds, a phrase taken from the Greek, meaning “to go down.” These winds do exactly that: Pulled by gravity, they descend rapidly from the tops of hills and mountains, scouring snow from higher elevations and depositing it below.
  The researchers modeled how such winds could have built up snow above the tent, and how long it would have taken to reach a critical load that would cause the top slab to slip off the weaker layer below, now that its structural integrity was compromised by the cut. “This was how the loading was increasing,” says Gaume. “Because there was no other way—there was no snowfall on that night.” Sometime after midnight, enough weight had built above the weak layer that it suddenly collapsed, sending the slab into the tent. It would have been a relatively small avalanche—maybe 16 feet by 16 feet—which the researchers simulated with inspiration from the Disney snow model. It would have been enough to fill the hole the campers had dug into the snow, but not enough that the rescue team would be able to find clear signs of an avalanche 26 days later.
  An avalanche doesn’t need to be large, though, to cause grave damage to the human body. Typically, hikers who get caught up in one are likely to just suffocate. But in this case, none of the nine victims died of suffocation, and some had severe chest and head trauma.
 This, too, can be explained by the dynamics of the slab avalanche and the downward winds. While it wasn’t snowing at the time of the incident, the katabatic winds would have produced a much more dangerous kind of deposit above the tent. “The wind was eroding and transporting the snow, which was made of very small crystals,” says Gaume. “And then when it deposits, [the crystals] are highly compacted.” This could have created a dense slab of snow that weighed perhaps 25 pounds per cubic foot. And even more unfortunate for our adventurers, they’d laid their skis out as a floor for their tent, creating a hard substrate for the snow to crush them against.
 Gaume and Puzrin went even further by modeling what this trauma could have looked like. To calibrate their simulation, they used data from old automotive industry crash tests done using human cadavers, rather than dummies. (To be fair, it was the 1970s, which was a … different time.) They then modeled the release of simulated snow blocks of different sizes onto a digital model of a human body, and compared that to the crash test results. “What we saw is that it would not be fatal, but it would create moderate-to-serious injuries,” says Gaume. (Below, you can see the damage a chunk of snow a meter across could do.)
  From this, they concluded that the mountaineers survived the initial crush of snow, cutting their way out of the tent, although some of them were seriously injured. But if they’d escaped a relatively small avalanche, why would they flee over half a mile away, instead of sticking around to dig out their supplies, especially their boots? Investigators found the group had actually stashed another set of supplies in the forest, so perhaps they’d set out for them in a panic. “You start to cut the tent from the inside to get out,” says Gaume. “You see there was an avalanche, and then you might be afraid of a  second avalanche. And so they may have decided that the best option would probably be to go to the forest, make a fire, and try to find the supply.”
  But, clad in little clothing, they didn’t survive more than a few hours in the biting cold. They had no way of knowing the intricate dynamics of a slab avalanche, and that, shocked as they were, it may have been safe to dig out their supplies and move along.
 “It really does look to me that an avalanche is the most plausible explanation of the situation,” says Jordy Hendrikx, a snow avalanche scientist at Montana State University, who wasn’t involved in this new work. “I think it's exciting that someone like Johan [Gaume], with the skill set that he has and the way that he's developed his models, can give us some more insights on that.”
  Back in 1959, investigators had a more limited understanding of how avalanches work. They didn’t realize, for instance, that under the right conditions, it’s perfectly reasonable for an avalanche to slide on a slope of less than 30 degrees. So for a while, authorities suspected local peoples of murdering the adventurers. And if an avalanche didn’t seem particularly plausible, why not consider the supernatural? “I think aliens and Yeti are fun ideas,” says Hendrikx. “And especially given the time, the Soviet Union and, even more recently, Russia, aren’t known for transparency in their information. So I can understand why these wild theories have come together.”
 While this new slab avalanche theory actually has scientific rigor to it, it remains exactly that: a theory. After all, no eyewitnesses survived. “I want to be clear that we do not claim that we solve the mystery,” says Gaume. “I mean,  no one wants this mystery to be solved in Russia. This is part of the Russian lore.”

title: 俄罗斯冒险家神秘地冻死了。 新理论解释了为什么
title_en: Russian Adventurers Mysteriously Froze to Death. A New Theory Explains Why

 Rocky Linux将成为Ctrl IQ收入的受益者，而不是其来源-该公司在公告中将自己描述为集成了企业，超大规模，云的关键功能的完整技术堆栈的供应商和高性能计算。＆＃34;
   如果您在过去的几个月中一直躲在Linux的岩石下，那么CentOS Linux是Red Hat Enterprise Linux最为广泛使用的克隆。 Kurtzer在2004年与导师Rocky McGaugh共同创立了CentOS Linux，并独立运营了10年，直到2014年被Red Hat收购。当Red Hat在一个备受争议的2020年12月公告中杀死了CentOS Linux时，Kurtzer立即宣布了他的意图。用新的发行版重新创建CentOS，以他的已故导师的名字命名。 Rocky Linux的概念引起了社区的积极响应，但是在创建和维护Linux发行版方面却花费了大量的工作和金钱。 CentOS Linux项目本身在2014年收购Red Hat时就明确表示了这一点。没有自己的资金来源，Rocky Linux成为完全1：1替代产品（为CentOS所服务的大量用户提供服务）的几率似乎最好。
  通过流行语宾果游戏，Ctrl IQ的真正业务似乎是为高性能计算（HPC）工作负载提供相对完整的基础架构，这些基础架构能够跨多个站点和/或云提供商运行。
    创建了Ctrl IQ完全支持的技术堆栈，以加快工作流程的多Prem，多云，多体系结构编排的速度和效率。该解决方案适合现有的企业架构和基础架构，并将包括：
 Rocky Linux：由Ctrl IQ首席执行官兼CentOS Warewulf创始人Kurtzer创建的，支持云和企业的企业Linux：旨在管理计算资源集群的可扩展系统管理套件。 Ctrl计算堆栈：完全受支持的计算堆栈，适用于所有性能密集型工作流程，例如HPC，AI，ML和科学工作流程。 Fuzzball：在托管或托管的内部部署解决方案中提供智能，安全且高效的业务流程和数据编排。 Ctrl IQ Cloud：提供一个安全的混合平台，以执行和协调对性能至关重要的工作流和服务。
 Rocky Linux本身，Ctrl Computing Stacks，Fuzzball和Ctrl IQ Cloud仍然比下载更富梦想，但并不是所有Ctrl IQ产品都是理论上的。同样由Kurtzer创立的Warewulf，目前由美国能源部开发和维护。任何人都可以自由下载和使用Warewulf，但不难想象在与其中一位创始人进行咨询时会带来增值。

 不，它不是全资拥有，仍然基于社区。 Ctrl IQ支持费用，法律费用等的初始资金，[Rocky Linux]是完整技术堆栈的OS选项。
 Ctrl IQ是Rocky Linux项目，Amazon Web Services（提供核心构建基础结构）和Mattermost（提供企业协作服务）的三个一级赞助商之一，Thomas＆＃34; Slack，但不提供Slack。 ＃34;
 通常，Rocky Linux预计将在2021年第二季度广泛使用，第一版候选版本预计在3月31日发布。
note_en:
Gregory Kurtzer, co-founder of the now-defunct CentOS Linux distribution, has founded a new startup company called  Ctrl IQ which will serve in part as a sponsoring company for the upcoming  Rocky Linux distribution.
 Rocky Linux is to be a benefactor of Ctrl IQ's revenue, not its source—the company describes itself in its  announcement as the suppliers of a "full technology stack integrating key capabilities of enterprise, hyper-scale, cloud and high-performance computing."
   If you've been hiding under a Linux rock for the last few months, CentOS Linux was the most widely known and used clone of Red Hat Enterprise Linux. Kurtzer co-founded CentOS Linux in 2004 with mentor Rocky McGaugh, and it operated independently for 10 years until being acquired by Red Hat in 2014. When Red Hat killed off CentOS Linux in a highly controversial December 2020 announcement, Kurtzer immediately announced his intention to recreate CentOS with a new distribution, named after his deceased mentor. The Rocky Linux concept got immediate, positive community reaction—but there's an awful lot of work and expense that goes into creating and maintaining a Linux distribution. The CentOS Linux project itself made that clear when it went for the Red Hat acquisition in 2014; without its own source of funding, the odds of Rocky Linux becoming a complete 1:1 replacement—serving the same massive volume of users that CentOS did—seemed dicey at best.
  Wading through the buzzword bingo, Ctrl IQ's real business seems to be in supplying relatively turn-key infrastructure for high-performance computing (HPC) workloads, capable of running distributed across multiple sites and/or cloud providers.
    Ctrl IQ's fully supported technology stack was created to accelerate speed and efficiency of multi-prem, multi-cloud, multi-architecture orchestration of workflows. The solution fits within existing enterprise architecture and infrastructures, and will include:
 Rocky Linux: enterprise Linux supported for cloud and enterprise created by Kurtzer, Ctrl IQ CEO and founder of CentOS  Warewulf: scalable systems management suite designed to manage clusters of compute resources.  Ctrl Computing Stacks: fully supported computational stacks designed for all performance intensive workflows like, HPC, AI, ML and scientific workflows.  Fuzzball: provides the intelligent, secure and performant orchestration of workflows and data in a managed or hosted on-premise solution.  Ctrl IQ Cloud: provides a secure hybrid platform to execute and orchestrate both performance critical workflows and services.
 Rocky Linux itself is still more dream than download, as are Ctrl Computing Stacks, Fuzzball, and Ctrl IQ Cloud—but not all of Ctrl IQ's offerings are theoretical.  Warewulf, also founded by Kurtzer, is currently developed and maintained by the US Department of Energy. Anyone can freely download and use Warewulf, but it's not difficult to imagine value added in consulting with one of its founders.
 Kurtzer and his new company received accolades from Sandia National Laboratories—the current home of Warewulf—as well as from investment firm IAG, which also funds (and sees Ctrl IQ's mission as critical to) NAS company  OpenDrives.
  We asked Ctrl IQ what the relationship between itself and Rocky Linux would be—specifically, whether the Rocky Linux project would operate separately and independently or as a wholly owned property of Ctrl IQ. A Ctrl IQ representative confirmed that Rocky Linux is and will remain a community project.
 No, it is not wholly owned and is still community-based. Ctrl IQ is supporting the initial capital for expenses, legal, etc. and [Rocky Linux] is an OS option for the full tech stack.
 Ctrl IQ is one of three Tier 1 sponsors identified by the Rocky Linux project, along with Amazon Web Services (which provides core build infrastructure) and Mattermost, which is providing enterprise collaboration services—think "Slack, but not Slack."
 Rocky Linux is generally expected to be widely available in Q2 2021, with a first-release candidate build  expected on March 31.
title: Rocky Linux获400万美元A轮融资的母公司
title_en: Rocky Linux gets a parent company, with $4m Series A funding

对于YC支持的Aviron来说，这是发展的理想时机。这家位于多伦多的初创公司一直为B2B市场提供游戏化划船机-专门用于酒店和公寓楼等人流量大的场所。它仍然是一家规模很小的公司，只有10名员工，迄今为止已筹集了约75万美元。
 突然，该公司发现自己试图与Peloton等技术巨头竞争市场份额。
  当然，到目前为止，Aviron自己的销售额比自行车巨人的谦逊得多。到目前为止，该公司在很大程度上一直依靠口口相传，自7月份面向消费者市场推出以来，该公司已售出1000台划船机。该设备的零售价为每件2299美元-尽管您可以在网上找到价格更低的设备。
 该公司与ODM合作创建机器。 Aivron的主要特色是软件，特别是通过内置显示屏提供的联网游戏体验，同时它还带来了一些不错的效果，例如安静的尼龙皮带和100磅的自动电子电阻。每月的订阅费用为20到30美元，该公司很快会注意到您可以随时取消。
  创始人兼首席执行官Andy Hoang告诉TechCrunch，“挥舞着您的85％的肌肉。” “影响很小。有很多好处，但是却很无聊，也很艰难。当您将其与高强度训练结合使用时，您将拥有一台几乎不需要做的死亡机器。有什么比在上面放视频游戏更好的娱乐方式呢？”
 该系统具有六种不同的锻炼类别，包括与其他赛艇运动员的实时比赛。有一些介绍性锻炼，以确保初学者不会因为直接跳入竞争性划船而受伤，但是总的来说，该系统避免了Peloton式的上课。
 “我们的训练很简短，” Hoang说。 “他们大概需要10到15分钟。您可能会做一两个，到最后，您会觉得自己要死了，因为它是如此的艰难。细气管球通常为40-60分钟，强度略低，阻力较小。显然，这是一个由讲师指导的课程，而不是被僵尸追赶。”
note_en:
For YC-backed  Aviron, it was the ideal time to pivot. The Toronto-based startup had been providing gamified rowing machines for the B2B market – specifically for use in high traffic settings like hotels and apartment buildings. It’s still a small operation with 10 employees and around $750,000 raised to date.
 Suddenly the company found itself in attempting to compete for market share against tech giants like Peloton.
  Of course, thus far Aviron’s own sales are considerably more humble than the cycling giant. Until now, the company has largely relied on word of mouth sales, having sold in the neighborhood of 1,000 rowing machines since launching for the consumer market in July. The equipment retails for $2,299 a piece – though you can find it online for less.
 The company works with an ODM to create the machine. And while it touts some nice touches like a quiet nylon belt and 100-pounds of automatic electronic resistance, Aivron’s main differentiator is the software – specially a connected gaming experience via the built-in display. The monthly subscription runs $20-$30 and the company is quick to note that you can cancel at any time.
  “Rowing engages 85% of your muscles,” founder and CEO Andy Hoang tells TechCrunch. “It’s low impact. There are a ton of benefits, but it’s super boring and super tough. When you combine it with high-intensity training, you have a death machine that pretty much no’s gonna want to do. What better way to make it fun and exciting than by putting video games on there?”
 The system sports six different workout categories, including real time competition with other rowers. There are a few introductory workouts, to ensure that first-timers don’t injure themselves by just jumping directly into competitive rowing, but on the whole, the system avoids Peloton-style classes.
 “Our workouts are short,” says Hoang. “They’re like 10-15 minutes. You do maybe one or two of them, and by the end of it, you feel like you’re going to die because it’s so tough. Peloton is typically 40-60 minutes, a little bit lower intensity and with less resistance. And obviously it’s a class led by an instructor, rather than getting chased by zombies.”
